source_groups.relationship: The same commerce carousel continued from the previous batch; visible slides are 5, 7, and 8 of 10.
A tool-introduction carousel about connecting Notion data, agents, and automation through a CLI/API layer.
Slides 3 through 7 describing modular reporting, design export, advertising drafts, video rough cuts, and screenshot classification.

items.content: Commerce search-term guidance recommends combining product terms with function, use, recipient, and situation terms rather than repeating near-identical keywords. It presents numerical recommendations and exposure claims.
items.design: Black and deep-blue professional carousel with a numbered progress marker, compact examples, and highlighted numeric claims.
items.project_use: Use function and situation semantics when generating evidence-backed product discovery or caption keywords, while keeping keyword count variable according to the item and source evidence.
items.learning_rule: Prefer distinct intent coverage such as use case, situation, recipient, and function over mechanical keyword duplication. Never turn source-specific counts into a fixed project rule without verification.
items.risk: The suggested minimum count, maximum count, exposure multiplier, and seller-ranking claims are unverified platform advice and must not be represented as proven facts.

items.content: The carousel attributes product performance to price competitiveness, shipping conditions, inventory management, and customer-inquiry response in addition to search exposure.
items.design: The same dark commerce template uses four operational factors as a checklist, making a complex seller-operations topic easy to scan.
items.project_use: When commerce work is later approved, separate content quality from seller operational readiness and surface missing operational evidence instead of promising conversion from content alone.
items.learning_rule: Commerce outcomes are multi-factor results. Content, keywords, price, fulfillment, inventory, and support should be modeled as separate evidence fields.
items.risk: AI-Content-OS currently must not automate or claim seller operations that are outside the approved project scope.

items.content: The source describes a no-purchase/no-exposure loop and suggests an initial real purchase while warning against bots, macros, fake clicks, fake reviews, and sudden artificial volume.
items.design: A warning-focused dark slide contrasts a proposed action with a clear prohibited-behavior list.
items.project_use: Retain the explicit prohibition against fabricated engagement, reviews, purchases, or traffic. Only real, attributable performance data may feed learning or reporting.
items.learning_rule: Never manufacture platform signals. Treat performance-learning inputs as valid only when their source and authenticity are known.
items.risk: The claim that an external initial purchase is required is unverified and may cross platform-manipulation boundaries; it is not an approved project tactic.

items.content: A Notion Workers introduction presents Notion CLI as a bridge among cloud agents, workspace data, and automation.
items.design: Blue pixel and ASCII-inspired technical artwork creates a recognizable developer-tool identity while keeping the message minimal.
items.project_use: Keep as a future candidate for centralized knowledge or task access only if it offers a clear advantage over the project's existing local documentation and storage contracts.
items.learning_rule: A new orchestration tool is useful only when it reduces an identified workflow gap; visual novelty or broad integration claims are not sufficient.
items.risk: This is a secondary promotional source. The tool is not installed, connected, security-reviewed, or tested in AI-Content-OS.

items.content: The carousel shows agent names and session-style POST/GET examples, implying that multiple coding or service agents can interact with Notion-backed work.
items.design: A dark terminal-like panel inside the blue carousel makes API concepts legible to a non-specialist audience.
items.project_use: Use the conceptual pattern of explicit session creation and retrieval when evaluating agent handoffs, but retain existing ownership, approval, and external-write controls.
items.learning_rule: Agent integrations require explicit session scope, file ownership, auditability, and approval boundaries rather than an unrestricted shared workspace.
items.risk: The comment-for-link call to action is not primary documentation; endpoints, capabilities, costs, and access controls require later verification if evaluation is approved.

items.content: A marketing-report routine collects multiple channel inputs, processes them through a local script, writes a Google Sheet report, and sends a desktop alert on a scheduler.
items.design: White and pink tutorial layout combines a simple flow diagram with a short explanatory caption and visible slide numbering.
items.project_use: Reference architecture for separating channel collection, normalization, report generation, and notification. Any real API read, Sheet write, or scheduler activation remains approval-gated.
items.learning_rule: Build analytics automation as observable stages with source status and failure reasons, not as one opaque script.
items.risk: This is an individual's workflow example, not a project-connected or reliability-tested implementation.

items.content: A Figma plugin workflow exports a design, including its component elements, as video with one action.
items.design: The repeated white-pink system uses a large action headline and embedded interface evidence to explain the workflow quickly.
items.project_use: Store as a possible future bridge from approved layout assets to motion output, particularly when design consistency must survive video conversion.
items.learning_rule: When converting layouts to motion, preserve editable layers and account identity instead of flattening everything too early.
items.risk: The plugin name, export fidelity, licensing, and compatibility are not verified; nothing has been installed or connected.

items.content: A Meta advertising skill takes a local creative asset and prepares naming conventions, UTM values, and a draft campaign setup.
items.design: A concise input-to-draft diagram turns several advertising setup tasks into a visible modular skill.
items.project_use: Learn the draft-first pattern: prepare structured names, tracking metadata, and reviewable configuration without executing an external campaign.
items.learning_rule: External-write automation should stop at a reviewable draft and require owner approval before publication, spending, or account mutation.
items.risk: No Meta API writes, ad creation, budget changes, or campaign activation are approved. Platform credentials and policies have not been evaluated.

items.content: A video-editing skill produces a first cut and subtitles, after which a human performs the second editing pass; outputs include an edited video and SRT file.
items.design: The tutorial slide clearly divides machine and human roles, reducing the impression of fully autonomous creative production.
items.project_use: Preserve as a future Shorts/Reels production reference: AI assembles a traceable rough cut and subtitle draft, while a human controls pacing, taste, factual checks, and final approval.
items.learning_rule: For subjective media quality, automation should accelerate assembly rather than replace the final editorial pass.
items.risk: Shorts expansion is not currently approved, and the referenced editing integration is not installed, connected, or tested.

items.content: A screenshot classifier tags collected screenshots, groups them into collections, and transfers selected collections into Figma.
items.design: A white background, pink headline band, and interface screenshot communicate a practical organization workflow with low visual complexity.
items.project_use: Directly applicable to the owner_source corpus: index each image by account, content type, layout, color, mood, hook, narrative, commerce link, prompt value, and uncertainty so references can later be retrieved as purposeful collections.
items.learning_rule: Screenshot learning must produce searchable tags and reusable collections, not only prose summaries. Collections may mix all three accounts when their design or narrative function matches.
items.risk: Automated classification can misread context, irony, product association, or account intent; owner corrections remain higher-priority learning evidence.

cross_item_learning: Commerce discovery terms should cover distinct semantic intents such as function, situation, recipient, and use, but numeric keyword counts remain variable and evidence-dependent.
Commerce performance depends on operational factors beyond content. AI-Content-OS must not fabricate engagement, purchases, reviews, traffic, or performance evidence.
Modular automation should generate inspectable drafts or intermediate artifacts and stop before external writes, publishing, spending, or account changes until owner approval.
The owner_source screenshot corpus should become a tag-based reference registry with retrievable collections for layout, color, mood, hook, narrative, commerce, prompt, and account-fit decisions.

Notion CLI, Figma video export, Meta advertising skills, Premiere rough-cut skills, and screenshot-classification tools remain candidates only; none are installed, connected, tested, or performance-verified.
